name: Victory Garden Movement
description: The Victory Garden Movement, also known as war gardens or food gardens for defense, was a significant initiative during both World War I and World War II. It encouraged citizens to grow their own fruits and vegetables in private residences and public parks to supplement food supplies and reduce pressure on the national food system. Proponents argued that these gardens not only provided food security but also fostered a sense of national unity, patriotism, and self-reliance during times of crisis. They believed these gardens were a tangible way for civilians to contribute to the war effort. During WWI and WWII, propaganda heavily promoted victory gardens. Some proponents claim that the movement demonstrated the power of collective action and the potential for individuals to become more self-sufficient and resilient, especially in the face of governmental or societal collapse. Some fringe theories suggest the government covertly used the movement to control the food supply and promote specific dietary habits. Mainstream historical perspective acknowledges the significant impact of victory gardens on food production and morale during wartime. Historians emphasize the role of government agencies, community organizations, and media campaigns in promoting the movement. While acknowledging the positive contributions, scholarly analyses also examine the social and economic factors that influenced participation in victory gardening, including class, race, and geographic location. Critiques focus on the romanticized view of self-sufficiency, noting that victory gardens were often dependent on existing infrastructure and resources. Furthermore, the long-term impact of the movement on food systems and individual behavior is debated, with some arguing that its legacy has been largely forgotten.
subtitle: Homegrown food for national strength.

claims.claim: Victory Gardens significantly supplemented national food supplies during wartime.
claims.evidence: Documented increases in fruit and vegetable production during both World Wars; reports from government agencies like the USDA.
claims.criticism: While significant, the exact percentage of food produced is debated; some argue it was more symbolic than substantial, with commercial agriculture still playing the dominant role.

claims.claim: Victory Gardens fostered a sense of national unity and patriotism.
claims.evidence: Propaganda posters and films promoting victory gardens; anecdotal evidence of community involvement and shared gardening experiences.
claims.criticism: Patriotism can be performative; motivations for gardening varied widely, including economic necessity and personal enjoyment; not everyone participated.

claims.claim: Victory Gardens demonstrated the potential for individual self-sufficiency and resilience.
claims.evidence: Personal accounts of gardeners successfully growing their own food; examples of communities organizing to share resources and knowledge.
claims.criticism: True self-sufficiency is difficult to achieve; victory gardens often relied on purchased seeds, tools, and fertilizers; success depended on access to land, water, and expertise.

keyFigures.name: Charles Lathrop Pack
keyFigures.role: President of the National War Garden Commission during WWI.

keyFigures.name: Claude Wickard
keyFigures.role: Secretary of Agriculture during WWII, promoted Victory Gardens.

keyFigures.name: Amy Bentley
keyFigures.role: Historian specializing in food history, offers critical perspectives on the movement.

timeline.event: National War Garden Commission promotes gardening during WWI.
Victory Garden campaign intensifies during WWII.
Resurgence of interest in home gardening and local food systems, sometimes linked to the Victory Garden movement.

sources.author: Amy Bentley
sources.work: Eating for Victory: Food Rationing and the Politics of Domesticity
sources.title: Amy Bentley
